revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]][[古墳#薄葬令]]へのリダイレクト
revision.text: #REDIRECT[[古墳#薄葬令]]

'''薄葬令'''(はくそうれい)は、大化2年（646年）、身分に応じて墳墓の規模などを制限した[[勅令]]。
『[[日本書紀]]』大化2年（646年）3月22日条によれば、[[大化の改新]]のなかで薄葬令が規定された。 中国の故事に習い、民衆の犠牲を軽減するため、王臣と庶民の身分に応じて作ってよい陵墓を制限し、人馬の殉死殉葬を禁止し、 天皇の陵にかける時間を7日以内に制限するなどの制限が加えられた。 墳稜は小型簡素化され、前方後円墳の造営がなくなり、[[古墳時代]]は事実上終わりを告げる。
== さまざまな解釈 ==
中央集権化の一環であるとする解釈：大化の改新などにより大和朝廷は 地方豪族を押さえ中央集権国家へと変貌していった。 薄葬令は、地方豪族の権力の象徴と言える古墳の造営を制限するものであり、 全ての土地と人民は天皇に帰属するとした[[公地公民制]]の推進と関係がある。 よって、一部の支配者層は、古墳の造営を続けることが出来た。
後付けであるとする解釈：簡易な葬送について、「以前から出されていた薄葬令に従うもので問題はない」との 説明が付くように、後世の支配者が日本書紀に盛り込んだルールである。 例えば、[[持統天皇]]は703年に崩御し薄葬だった。彼女は天皇で初めて火葬され、 自身の墳稜を持たず夫の[[天武天皇]]の墓に合葬された。しかしそれ以前の、654年に崩御した[[孝徳天皇]]は薄葬ではない。
{{DEFAULTSORT:はくそうれい}}

'''薄葬令'''（はくそうれい）は、[[大化]]2年（[[646年]]）、身分に応じて墳墓の規模などを制限した[[勅令]]。
『[[日本書紀]]』大化2年（646年）3月22日条によれば、[[大化の改新]]のなかで薄葬令が規定された。 中国の故事に習い、民衆の犠牲を軽減するため、王臣と庶民の身分に応じて作ってよい陵墓を制限し、人馬の殉死殉葬を禁止し、天皇の陵にかける時間を7日以内に制限するなどの制限が加えられた。 墳稜は小型簡素化され、前方後円墳の造営がなくなり、[[古墳時代]]は事実上終わりを告げる。
== さまざまな解釈 == 中央集権化の一環であるとする解釈：大化の改新などにより大和朝廷は地方豪族を押さえ中央集権国家へと変貌していった。 薄葬令は、地方豪族の権力の象徴と言える古墳の造営を制限するものであり、全ての土地と人民は天皇に帰属するとした[[公地公民制]]の推進と関係がある。よって、一部の支配者層は、古墳の造営を続けることが出来た。
後付けであるとする解釈：簡易な葬送について、「以前から出されていた薄葬令に従うもので問題はない」との説明が付くように、後世の支配者が日本書紀に盛り込んだルールである。例えば、[[持統天皇]]は703年に崩御し薄葬だった。彼女は天皇で初めて火葬され、自身の墳稜を持たず夫の[[天武天皇]]の墓に合葬された。しかしそれ以前の、654年に崩御した[[孝徳天皇]]は薄葬ではない。

『[[日本書紀]]』大化2年（646年）3月22日条によれば、[[大化の改新]]のなかで薄葬令が規定された。 中国の故事に習い、民衆の犠牲を軽減するため、王臣と庶民の身分に応じて作ってよい陵墓を制限し、人馬の殉死殉葬を禁止し、天皇の陵にかける時間を7日以内に制限するなどの制限が加えられた。 墳陵は小型簡素化され、前方後円墳の造営がなくなり、[[古墳時代]]は事実上終わりを告げる。
== さまざまな解釈 == 中央集権化の一環であるとする解釈：大化の改新などにより大和朝廷は地方豪族を押さえ中央集権国家へと変貌していった。 薄葬令は、地方豪族の権力の象徴と言える古墳の造営を制限するものであり、全ての土地と人民は天皇に帰属するとした[[公地公民制]]の推進と関係がある。よって、一部の支配者層は、古墳の造営を続けることが出来た。
後付けであるとする解釈：簡易な葬送について、「以前から出されていた薄葬令に従うもので問題はない」との説明が付くように、後世の支配者が日本書紀に盛り込んだルールである。例えば、[[持統天皇]]は703年に崩御し薄葬だった。彼女は天皇で初めて火葬され、自身の墳陵を持たず夫の[[天武天皇]]の墓に合葬された。しかしそれ以前の、654年に崩御した[[孝徳天皇]]は薄葬ではない。
== 中国大陸 == 中国大陸では[[後漢]]末期（[[三国時代 (中国)|三国時代]]）の建安10年（[[205年]]）、[[曹操]]が薄葬令を発布している<ref>『[[宋書]]』礼志</ref>。

『[[日本書紀]]』大化2年（646年）3月22日条によれば、[[大化の改新]]のなかで薄葬令が規定された。 中国の故事に習い、民衆の犠牲を軽減するため、王臣と庶民の身分に応じて作ってよい陵墓を制限し、人馬の殉死殉葬を禁止し、天皇の陵にかける時間を7日以内に制限するなどの制限が加えられた。 墳陵は小型簡素化され、前方後円墳の造営がなくなり、[[古墳時代]]は事実上終わりを告げる。
== さまざまな解釈 == 中央集権化の一環であるとする解釈：大化の改新などにより大和朝廷は地方豪族を押さえ中央集権国家へと変貌していった。 薄葬令は、地方豪族の権力の象徴と言える古墳の造営を制限するものであり、全ての土地と人民は天皇に帰属するとした[[公地公民制]]の推進と関係がある。よって、一部の支配者層は、古墳の造営を続けることが出来た。
後付けであるとする解釈：簡易な葬送について、「以前から出されていた薄葬令に従うもので問題はない」との説明が付くように、後世の支配者が日本書紀に盛り込んだルールである。例えば、[[持統天皇]]は703年に崩御し薄葬だった。彼女は天皇で初めて火葬され、自身の墳陵を持たず夫の[[天武天皇]]の墓に合葬された。しかしそれ以前の、654年に崩御した[[孝徳天皇]]は薄葬ではない。
== 中国大陸 == 中国大陸では[[後漢]]末期（[[三国時代 (中国)|三国時代]]）の建安10年（[[205年]]）、[[曹操]]が薄葬令を発布している<ref>『[[宋書]]』礼志</ref>。
== 脚注 == <references />
{{デフォルトソート:はくそうれい}} [[Category:日本の歴史上の法]] [[Category:命令文書]] [[Category:7世紀の法]] [[Category:日本の古代法]] [[Category:646年の日本]]

{{出典の明記|date=2016年2月7日 (日) 01:11 (UTC)}} '''薄葬令'''（はくそうれい）とは、[[大化]]2年（[[646年]]）に発布された、身分に応じて墳墓の規模などを制限した[[勅令]]。
[[大化の改新]]の一環とされ、「大化の薄葬令」とも呼ばれる。墳陵は小型簡素化され、[[前方後円墳]]の造営がなくなり、[[古墳時代]]は事実上終わりを告げる。
== 概要 == 『[[日本書紀]]』大化2年（646年）3月22日条にある一連の詔を「薄葬令」と呼ぶ。
中国の故事に習い、民衆の犠牲を軽減するため、王臣と庶民の身分に応じて作ってよい陵墓を制限し、人馬の殉死殉葬を禁止し、天皇の陵にかける時間を7日以内に制限するという趣旨の[[詔]]が発せられた。
== 解説 == 初期の[[大和政権]]は、権力の象徴としての巨大な古墳を必要としたが、[[継体天皇]]の時代以降に徐々に大王を中心とする中央集権制が進んでいくと、権力の象徴としての古墳を作る意味が次第に無くなって行き、古墳は姿を消し始める<ref>『知識ゼロからの古墳入門』[[広瀬和雄]]、幻冬舎、2015年、p.138</ref>。特に593年に[[推古天皇]]の[[摂政]]に就任した[[聖徳太子]]が、[[冠位十二階]]や[[十七条憲法]]などの法を整備したことがきっかけで、畿内では7世紀始めまでには巨大古墳の築造は終了する。
その後も東国においては権力の象徴としての巨大古墳が必要とされたようで、もうしばらく巨大古墳の築造が続くが、これも7世紀初めには築造を終了する。
巨大な前方後円墳の築造終了後は、より小規模な方墳・円墳が主流となり、大王もそれに従うことになる。7世紀中ごろには中国の思想が導入され、大王は天下八方の支配者にふさわしい形である[[八角墳]]に葬られるようになるが、7世紀末には仏教の思想による[[火葬]]が導入され、[[文武天皇]]が火葬の後に八角墓に葬られたのを最後に、古墳の築造は終了する。
== さまざまな解釈 == 薄葬令が発布されたとされる646年に完全に古墳の築造がストップしたわけではなく、その後もしばらく古墳が築造されている。これに関していくつか解釈が存在する。基本的に、日本書紀における「大化の改新」の記述には後世の潤色があると考えられており、「薄葬令」の詔も日本書紀が編纂された時点での「後付け」であるとの説が主流である。
中央集権化の一環であるとする解釈：大化の改新などにより大和朝廷は地方豪族を押さえ中央集権国家へと変貌していった。 薄葬令は、地方豪族の権力の象徴と言える古墳の造営を制限するものであり、全ての土地と人民は天皇に帰属するとした[[公地公民制]]の推進と関係がある。よって、一部の支配者層は、古墳の造営を続けることが出来た。
後付けであるとする解釈：簡易な葬送について、「以前から出されていた薄葬令に従うもので問題はない」との説明が付くように、後世の支配者が日本書紀に盛り込んだルールである。例えば、[[持統天皇]]は703年に崩御し薄葬だった。彼女は天皇で初めて火葬され、自身の墳陵を持たず夫の[[天武天皇]]の墓に合葬された。しかしそれ以前の、654年に崩御した[[孝徳天皇]]は薄葬ではない。
== 中国大陸 == 中国大陸では[[後漢]]末期（[[三国時代 (中国)|三国時代]]）の建安10年（[[205年]]）、[[曹操]]が薄葬令を発布している<ref>『[[宋書]]』礼志</ref>。
== 脚注 == <references />
{{デフォルトソート:はくそうれい}} [[Category:日本の歴史上の法]] [[Category:命令文書]] [[Category:7世紀の法]] [[Category:日本の古代法]] [[Category:646年の日本]]

{{出典の明記|date=2016年2月7日 (日) 01:11 (UTC)}} '''薄葬令'''（はくそうれい）とは、[[大化]]2年（[[646年]]）に発布された、身分に応じて墳墓の規模などを制限した[[勅令]]。
[[大化の改新]]の一環とされ、「大化の薄葬令」とも呼ばれる。墳陵は小型簡素化され、[[前方後円墳]]の造営がなくなり、[[古墳時代]]は事実上終わりを告げる。
== 概要 == 『[[日本書紀]]』大化2年（646年）3月22日条にある一連の詔を「薄葬令」と呼ぶ。
中国の故事に習い、民衆の犠牲を軽減するため、王臣と庶民の身分に応じて作ってよい陵墓を制限し、人馬の殉死殉葬を禁止し、天皇の陵にかける時間を7日以内に制限するという趣旨の[[詔]]が発せられた。
== 解説 == 初期の[[大和政権]]は、権力の象徴としての巨大な古墳を必要としたため、大王から地方豪族まで盛んに巨大古墳を築造したが、[[継体天皇]]の時代以降に徐々に大王を中心とする中央集権制が進んでいくと、権力の象徴としての古墳を作る意味が次第に無くなって行き、古墳は姿を消し始める<ref>『知識ゼロからの古墳入門』[[広瀬和雄]]、幻冬舎、2015年、p.138</ref>。特に593年に[[推古天皇]]の[[摂政]]に就任した[[聖徳太子]]が、[[冠位十二階]]や[[十七条憲法]]などの法を整備したことがきっかけで、畿内では7世紀始めまでには巨大古墳の築造は終了する。
その後もまだ東国においては大和朝廷の権力の象徴としての巨大な前方後円墳が必要とされたようで、巨大古墳を通じて東国の基盤を強化するためにも、畿内を凌駕する規模の巨大古墳の築造が続くが、これも7世紀初めには築造を終了する。
巨大な前方後円墳の築造終了後は、より小規模な方墳・円墳が主流となり、大王もそれに従うことになる。7世紀中ごろには中国の思想が導入され、大王は天下八方の支配者にふさわしい形である[[八角墳]]に葬られるようになるが、7世紀末には仏教の思想による[[火葬]]が導入され、[[文武天皇]]が火葬の後に八角墓に葬られたのを最後に、古墳の築造は終了する。

後付けであるとする解釈：簡易な葬送について、「以前から出されていた薄葬令に従うもので問題はない」との説明が付くように、後世の支配者が日本書紀に盛り込んだルールである。例えば、[[持統天皇]]は703年に崩御し薄葬だった。彼女は天皇で初めて火葬され、自身の墳陵を持たず夫の[[天武天皇]]の墓に合葬された。しかしそれ以前の、654年に崩御した[[孝徳天皇]]は薄葬ではない。
== 中国大陸 == 中国大陸では[[後漢]]末期（[[三国時代 (中国)|三国時代]]）の建安10年（[[205年]]）、[[曹操]]が薄葬令を発布している<ref>『[[宋書]]』礼志</ref>。

revision.comment: Created page with "{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank. It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時..."
revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of burial mounds based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato polity|ja|大和政権}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-Article Constitution|ja|十七条憲法}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. **Centralization Theory**: The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. **Post-facto Interpretation**: The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />

{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of burial mounds based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato polity|ja|大和政権}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-Article Constitution|ja|十七条憲法}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />

== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of burial mounds based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of burial mounds based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.

1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of burial mounds based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />

== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of burial mounds based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />

The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of burial mounds based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of burial mounds based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.

1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of burial mounds based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of burial mounds based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />

== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />

{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />

The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化|12=draft}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新|12=draft}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship|12=draft}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|12=draft}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子|12=draft}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法|12=draft}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=draft}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇|12=draft}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操|12=draft}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />
<!--Thin Burial Edict-->
<!--Thin Burial Edict-->
<!--Thin Burial Edict-->

{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化|12=draft|12=draft}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新|12=draft|12=draft}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship|12=draft|12=draft}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子|12=draft|12=draft}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法|12=draft|12=draft}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft|12=draft}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操|12=draft|12=draft}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

revision.comment: 17 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:薄葬令 up to rev 71427081
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2016年2月7日 (日) 01:11 (UTC)}} '''薄葬令'''（はくそうれい）とは、[[大化]]2年（[[646年]]）に発布された、身分に応じて墳墓の規模などを制限した[[勅令]]。
[[大化の改新]]の一環とされ、「大化の薄葬令」とも呼ばれる。墳陵は小型簡素化され、[[前方後円墳]]の造営がなくなり、[[古墳時代]]は事実上終わりを告げる。
== 概要 == 『[[日本書紀]]』大化2年（646年）3月22日条にある一連の詔を「薄葬令」と呼ぶ。
中国の故事に習い、民衆の犠牲を軽減するため、王臣と庶民の身分に応じて作ってよい陵墓を制限し、人馬の殉死殉葬を禁止し、天皇の陵にかける時間を7日以内に制限するという趣旨の[[詔]]が発せられた。
== 解説 == 初期の[[大和政権]]は、権力の象徴としての巨大な古墳を必要としたため、大王から地方豪族まで盛んに巨大古墳を築造したが、[[継体天皇]]の時代以降に徐々に大王を中心とする中央集権制が進んでいくと、権力の象徴としての古墳を作る意味が次第に無くなって行き、古墳は姿を消し始める<ref>『知識ゼロからの古墳入門』[[広瀬和雄]]、幻冬舎、2015年、p.138</ref>。特に593年に[[推古天皇]]の[[摂政]]に就任した[[聖徳太子]]が、[[冠位十二階]]や[[十七条憲法]]などの法を整備したことがきっかけで、畿内では7世紀始めまでには巨大古墳の築造は終了する。
その後もまだ東国においては大和朝廷の権力の象徴としての巨大な前方後円墳が必要とされたようで、巨大古墳を通じて東国の基盤を強化するためにも、畿内を凌駕する規模の巨大古墳の築造が続くが、これも7世紀初めには築造を終了する。
巨大な前方後円墳の築造終了後は、より小規模な方墳・円墳が主流となり、大王もそれに従うことになる。7世紀中ごろには中国の思想が導入され、大王は天下八方の支配者にふさわしい形である[[八角墳]]に葬られるようになるが、7世紀末には仏教の思想による[[火葬]]が導入され、 707年に崩御した[[文武天皇]]が火葬の後に八角墓に葬られたのを最後に、古墳の築造は終了する。
== さまざまな解釈 == 薄葬令が発布されたとされる646年に完全に古墳の築造がストップしたわけではなく、その後もしばらく古墳が築造されている。これに関していくつか解釈が存在する。基本的に、日本書紀における「大化の改新」の記述には後世の潤色があると考えられており、「薄葬令」の詔も日本書紀が編纂された時点での「後付け」であるとの説が主流である。
中央集権化の一環であるとする解釈：大化の改新などにより大和朝廷は地方豪族を押さえ中央集権国家へと変貌していった。 薄葬令は、地方豪族の権力の象徴と言える古墳の造営を制限するものであり、全ての土地と人民は天皇に帰属するとした[[公地公民制]]の推進と関係がある。よって、一部の支配者層は、古墳の造営を続けることが出来た。
後付けであるとする解釈：簡易な葬送について、「以前から出されていた薄葬令に従うもので問題はない」との説明が付くように、後世の支配者が日本書紀に盛り込んだルールである。例えば、[[持統天皇]]は703年に崩御し薄葬だった。彼女は天皇で初めて火葬され、自身の墳陵を持たず夫の[[天武天皇]]の墓に合葬された。しかしそれ以前の、654年に崩御した[[孝徳天皇]]は薄葬ではない。
== 中国大陸 == 中国大陸では[[後漢]]末期（[[三国時代 (中国)|三国時代]]）の建安10年（[[205年]]）、[[曹操]]が薄葬令を発布している<ref>『[[宋書]]』礼志</ref>。
== 脚注 == <references />
{{デフォルトソート:はくそうれい}} [[Category:日本の歴史上の法]] [[Category:命令文書]] [[Category:7世紀の法]] [[Category:日本の古代法]] [[Category:646年の日本]]

It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法|qq=draft|qq=draft}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操|qq=draft|qq=draft}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Keitai|qq=}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Shōtoku|qq=}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|qq=}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|ja|十七条憲法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventeen-article constitution|qq=}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|qq=}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō system|qq=}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Jitō|qq=}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|ja|曹操|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cao Cao|qq=}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social Rank.
It is considered part of the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]] and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], as the construction of large-scale [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|qq=}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], who became regent in 593, reforms such as the [[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] and the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-article constitution]] further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|qq=}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions.
