1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.

However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|qq=}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.

{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social Rank.
It is considered part of the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]] and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], as the construction of large-scale [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|qq=}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], who became regent in 593, reforms such as the [[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] and the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-article constitution]] further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|qq=}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />

2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social Rank.
It is considered part of the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]] and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], as the construction of large-scale [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマト王権}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], who became regent in 593, reforms such as the [[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] and the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-article constitution]] further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />

Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|qq=|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], who became regent in 593, reforms such as the [[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] and the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-article constitution]] further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

Under [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], who became regent in 593, reforms such as the [[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] and the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-article constitution]] further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11622174]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social Rank.
It is considered part of the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]] and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], as the construction of large-scale [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kinship|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|qq=|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], who became regent in 593, reforms such as the [[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] and the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-article constitution]] further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11622174]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social Rank.
It is considered part of the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]] and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], as the construction of large-scale [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kinship|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|qq=|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], who became regent in 593, reforms such as the [[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] and the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-article constitution]] further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.

In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薄葬令]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11622174]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social Rank.
It is considered part of the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]] and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], as the construction of large-scale [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kinship|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|qq=|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], who became regent in 593, reforms such as the [[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] and the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-article constitution]] further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薄葬令]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11622174]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social Rank.
It is considered part of the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]] and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], as the construction of large-scale [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kinship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], who became regent in 593, reforms such as the [[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] and the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-article constitution]] further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薄葬令]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11622174]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social Rank.
It is considered part of the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]] and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], as the construction of large-scale [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kinship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], who became regent in 593, reforms such as the [[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] and the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-article constitution]] further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

It is considered part of the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]] and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], as the construction of large-scale [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kinship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], who became regent in 593, reforms such as the [[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] and the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-article constitution]] further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.

1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.

1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薄葬令]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薄葬令]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薄葬令]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11622174]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social Rank.
It is considered part of the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]] and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], as the construction of large-scale [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kinship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], who became regent in 593, reforms such as the [[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] and the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-article constitution]] further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />

{{wikidata link|Q11622174}} [[Category:646 in Japan]] [[Category:7th century in law]] [[Category:Ancient Laws of Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Laws in Japanese History]] [[Category:Official order documents]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薄葬令]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薄葬令]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薄葬令]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11622174]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social Rank.
It is considered part of the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]] and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], as the construction of large-scale [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kinship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], who became regent in 593, reforms such as the [[:en:Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] and the [[:en:Seventeen-article constitution|Seventeen-article constitution]] further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]], who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), [[:en:Cao Cao|Cao Cao]] promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />

It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." == Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kinship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|en|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|WD=Q2448809|lt=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen-article constitution}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|WD=Q232026|lt=Empress Jitō}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|en|Cao Cao|WD=Q204077|lt=Cao Cao}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|WD=Q232026|lt=Empress Jitō|1=Empress Jitō}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|en|Cao Cao|WD=Q204077|lt=Cao Cao|1=Cao Cao}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薄葬令]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薄葬令]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薄葬令]]
{{Citation needed|date=February 7, 2016}} '''Hakusōrei''' (薄葬令) was an imperial edict issued in the second year of the {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika era|1=Taika (era)}} (646), limiting the scale of tombs according to social Rank.
It is considered part of the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms|1=Taika Reform}} and is also referred to as the "Taika Hakusōrei." The decree led to the downsizing and simplification of kofun (tombs), effectively marking the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}, as the construction of large-scale {{ill|Zenpōkōenfun|en|Zenpōkōenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpōkōenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} ceased.
== Overview == The term "Hakusōrei" refers to a series of edicts recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} under the entry for the second year of Taika (646), on the 22nd day of the third month.
Modeled on practices from Chinese history, the edicts aimed to reduce the burdens placed on the populace. They restricted the size of Kofuns based on the Rank of the deceased, prohibited the sacrifice of humans and animals for burial purposes, and limited the construction time for imperial tombs to within seven days.
== Analysis == In the early days of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|lt=Yamato polity|ja|大和政権|1=Yamato Kingship|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}}, large-scale kofun served as symbols of power. Rulers, from the great kings to regional chieftains, built massive tombs. However, as centralized governance developed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}} and later, the symbolic importance of kofun diminished, leading to a gradual decline in their construction.<ref>Hirose Kazuo, *Knowledge Zero Guide to Kofun*, Gentosha, 2015, p. 138.</ref>
Under {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}}, who became regent in 593, reforms such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|en|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|WD=Q2448809|lt=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}} and the {{ill|Seventeen-article constitution|en|Seventeen-article constitution|WD=Q197248|lt=Seventeen-article constitution|1=Seventeen-article constitution}} further reduced the need for large tombs. In the Kinai region, the construction of large kofun had largely ceased by the early 7th century.
However, in the eastern provinces, massive kofun continued to be built as symbols of Yamato authority, reinforcing the central government’s influence. Even these practices ended in the early 7th century, giving way to smaller square and round tombs. By the mid-7th century, Chinese influences led to the construction of {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=octagonal tombs|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, considered befitting rulers as masters of the world’s eight directions. The introduction of Buddhist cremation practices further influenced burial customs, with Emperor Monmu’s cremation in 707 marking the last use of octagonal tombs.
== Interpretations == The cessation of kofun construction did not immediately follow the issuance of the Hakusōrei in 646. Several interpretations exist regarding this:
1. '''Centralization Theory''': The Hakusōrei is viewed as part of the broader effort to centralize power during the Taika Reforms. By restricting the construction of kofun, which symbolized local authority, the Yamato court enforced the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}, wherein all land and people belonged to the emperor. Some elites, however, continued building kofun.
2. '''Post-facto Interpretation''': The simplified burial practices described in the Nihon Shoki may reflect later additions rather than contemporary practices. For instance, {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|WD=Q232026|lt=Empress Jitō|1=Empress Jitō}}, who died in 703, was cremated and not buried in her own tomb but interred with her husband, {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. In contrast, Emperor Kōtoku, who died in 654, was not subjected to simplified burial customs.
== In Mainland China == In mainland China, a similar edict was issued during the late Eastern Han period (Three Kingdoms era). In the 10th year of Jian'an (205), {{ill|Cao Cao|en|Cao Cao|WD=Q204077|lt=Cao Cao|1=Cao Cao}} promulgated an edict on frugal burials.<ref>*Song Shu* (Book of Song), Rituals Chapter</ref>
== References == <references />
